text: a gentle, orange glow. The simple lantern — just a glass container with some oil and a wick through the lid — was one of her only luxuries. She probably used it too often, but it burned slow and ensured she didn’t have to be alone in the darkness . Returning the lighter to its place, she studied the porcelain again, turning it over in her hand. She traced the design with her fingertip, marveling at the way the looping lines came together to create something so beautiful. What purpose did it serve? None. It simply…was. Like so many of the remnants she’d found of the old world, the decoration had no practical use, couldn’t have aided in the item’s function . For some reason, such items spoke to her. She knew they were frivolous. A waste of her time and energy to collect. But Lara couldn’t resist . She placed the shard on the uppermost shelf of her little case, amidst the other pointless trinkets she’d gathered over the years — all items that weren’t worth anyone’s time to steal. They were pleasing to Lara’s eye, and that was enough . When she turned away from her treasures to look upon the rest of her home, she was reminded of how little she’d accomplished today. There was the pathetic pallet upon which she slept, and the wooden crate beside it with her few articles of clothing. Hunger reintroduced itself, gnawing at her gut. There was no food here . Even the hollowness of her stomach wasn’t enough to distract Lara from the true emptiness inside her. She was alone. Tabitha was her only friend, her only family. Her big sister, mother, best friend. Tabitha had guided Lara, taught her, comforted her. And now… Now, there was no one . She had nothing . With a cry of frustration, she sat down on the sturdy crate and yanked off her worn boots. Rising, she lifted the crate, stuffed her boots beneath it, and slammed it back down. For a long time, she sat hunched forward, breathing heavily as she picked at the wooden planks with her fingers. Her body shook with the effort of keeping her tears at bay. Crying couldn’t help anything. It certainly wouldn’t earn her any food . From outside, the soft sound of the chime rose above the constant drone of the wind. Lara’s muscles eased as the music chased away her sorrow and frustration. Anger wouldn’t help any more than crying . She closed her eyes and listened to the music, conjuring a melody in her mind. Her body swayed with the notes. She couldn’t ease her hunger, but she could free her mind for a little while . Lara hummed, the sound filling her chest and lifting her heart. Standing, she lifted her arms and slid her fingers into her hair. She rocked her hips in time with the beat in her head, letting the music take her away. Letting her forget . She stepped forward blindly, knowing in her heart exactly where everything lay inside the shack. The worn cloth of her skirt brushed her legs, twisting and flaring with her movement. Night air swept through the doorway, caressing her flushed cheeks with a brisk, fleeting kiss . Lara danced, not once opening her eyes in fear she would be torn from her dreams and dragged back into a world of hunger, depravity, and dust . Chapter Two R onin crouched, pressing his back to the overturned bus. The dirt that had accumulated around and inside of it would stop the bullets fired by the two reavers, but it was only a matter of time before they flanked him and nullified his cover . “Your salvage, dustwalker!” one of them called, his voice underrun with a digital crackle. “It’s ours whether we end you or not !” At least one synth, then. Even if they were both bots, their first shots had missed, which meant they weren’t operating at peak performance. Ronin wasn’t either, after so many years in the Dust, but his optics were still fully functional . The reavers only had three main avenues of approach, if they chose to attack — around either side of the bus, or over the top. Ronin’s processors ran simulations on all three. There were countless potential variations, despite the limited paths, and none likely to result in minimal personal damage . Wind swept over the wasteland, rustling the tufts of brown grass growing randomly on the dunes and spattering Ronin with grit. Adjusting his audio receptors, he picked out the quiet crunch of dirt beneath the reavers’ boots as they approached. He estimated their positions based on the sound . Pushing up, he leapt atop the bus. The old, rusty metal bent and flaked beneath him, but held his weight as he snapped to his first target. The reaver was only twenty-three and one-eighth feet away; well within range for the armor-piercing rounds to do their work. Ronin’s first burst caught the reaver square in the chest and punched through the casing beneath his synthetic skin. He fell before taking a return shot, limbs spasming . The other reaver reacted in the half-second Ronin’s attack allowed it, firing his rifle. The first bullets hit Ronin as he pivoted. He shook with the impact, but kept his arms steady and squeezed the trigger . The reaver’s gun bucked wildly, climbing with its uncontrolled recoil as Ronin’s shots pierced the bot’s casing and hit its power cell. Licks of blue fire erupted from the holes for a fraction of an instant. With smoke curling out of it, the reaver’s arms and torso went limp, its legs locking it in an upright position . Ronin held his weapon at the ready and approached the other bot. It twitched on the ground, kicking up little clouds of dust. Motor functions compromised. It had likely sustained damage to its CPU.
